text: Oxidative stress can cause cellular damage and activate signaling cascades that result in programmed cell death, including apoptosis and autophagy. Increased production of free radicals, such as reactive oxygen species (ROS) and reactive nitrogen species (RNS), collectively RONS, and a weakened antioxidant defence system can be detrimental. When free radicals overwhelm antioxidants, the resulting oxidative stress can cause damage to DNA, including base damage; strand breaks; and mutation, as well as damage to vital cellular components, such as lipid peroxidation within the cellular and mitochondrial membranes. Sufficient oxidative damage to the cell can result in programmed cell death (Pacheco and Stock, 2013; Tian et al., 2017). Overwhelming DNA damage from oxidative stress can result in cell damage and death.
